  "conventions": {
    "commits": "Conventional commits with feat/fix/refactor prefixes; likely DCO signed based on enterprise context",
    "testing": "Unit tests colocated with modules using #[cfg(test)]; integration tests in tests/ directory; likely property-based testing for parsers",
    "linting": "rustfmt for formatting; clippy for linting; likely enforced in CI",
    "error_handling": "Result<T, E> pattern with custom error types; thiserror or anyhow for error propagation",
    "imports": "Module-based organization with clear public API boundaries; likely use of trait objects for abstraction"
  },
  "patterns": [
    "Trait-based abstraction for pluggable backends (forge providers)",
    "Builder pattern for configuration and client initialization",
    "Error wrapping with context using thiserror",
    "Mock implementations for testing external integrations",
    "Feature flags for optional dependencies"
  ],
  "related_issues": [
    {
      "number": 735,
      "title": "Multi-forge support architecture discussion",
      "relevance": "Foundational discussion on forge abstraction design"
    },
    {
      "number": 1205,
      "title": "feat(core): multi-forge support for GitLab, Gitea/Forgejo, and Azure DevOps",
      "relevance": "Target issue requesting GitLab, Gitea/Forgejo, and Azure DevOps support"
    }
  ],
  "constraints": [
    "Must maintain backward compatibility with existing GitHub integration",
    "API clients for each forge must be abstracted behind common trait",
    "Authentication mechanisms vary significantly across forges (tokens, OAuth, PAT)",
    "Rate limiting and pagination differ per forge",
    "Repository metadata schema differs across platforms",
    "Webhook payload formats are forge-specific"
  ],
  "test_coverage": "Existing test coverage likely includes GitHub API mocking; integration tests for repository discovery and metadata fetching; unit tests for authentication and error handling. New forge support requires parallel test suites for each provider.",
  "library_findings": [
    {
      "library": "reqwest",
      "version": "0.11+",
      "relevant_api": "async HTTP client with built-in retry and timeout support",
      "notes": "Likely used for all forge API calls; supports custom headers and authentication schemes"
    },
    {
      "library": "serde",
      "version": "1.0+",
      "relevant_api": "JSON serialization/deserialization with custom derive macros",
      "notes": "Used for API response parsing; each forge requires custom struct definitions for their unique JSON schemas"
    },
    {
      "library": "thiserror or anyhow",
      "version": "latest",
      "relevant_api": "Error type derivation and context propagation",
      "notes": "For unified error handling across multiple forge implementations"
    }
  ],
  "approaches": [
    {
      "name": "Trait-based Forge Abstraction Layer",
      "description": "Create a ForgeProvider trait defining common operations (list_repos, get_repo_metadata, create_webhook, etc.). Implement concrete types for GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, and Azure DevOps. Use dependency injection to select provider at runtime based on configuration. Each provider encapsulates its own HTTP client, authentication, and response parsing.",
      "pros": [
        "Clean separation of concerns with minimal coupling",
        "Easy to add new forges without touching existing code",
        "Testable with mock implementations",
        "Follows Rust idioms and trait-based design",
        "Allows per-forge feature flags for optional dependencies"
      ],
      "cons": [
        "Requires careful design of trait to capture all operations without being too generic",
        "Some forge-specific features may not fit cleanly into common interface",
        "Trait object overhead if using dynamic dispatch"
      ],
      "complexity": "medium",
      "files_touched": 12
    },
    {
      "name": "Adapter Pattern with Unified API Client",
      "description": "Create a single ForgeClient struct that wraps provider-specific clients. Use an enum to dispatch to the correct implementation. Each provider is a module with its own types and HTTP logic. The ForgeClient exposes a unified API that translates calls to provider-specific operations. Configuration determines which provider to instantiate.",
      "pros": [
        "Simpler than trait objects for common cases",
        "Explicit control flow with enum dispatch",
        "Easier to handle provider-specific edge cases",
        "Reduces cognitive load with concrete types"
      ],
      "cons": [
        "More boilerplate for each new provider",
        "ForgeClient becomes a large match statement if many operations",
        "Less idiomatic Rust pattern",
        "Harder to extend without modifying core client"
      ],
      "complexity": "medium",
      "files_touched": 14
    },
    {
      "name": "Modular Plugin System with Macro-driven Registration",
      "description": "Define a ForgePlugin trait and use procedural macros to auto-register implementations. Each forge (GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, Azure) is a separate module that implements ForgePlugin and registers itself via macro. A central registry maps forge type to provider. Configuration loads the appropriate plugin at startup. Allows compile-time or runtime plugin discovery.",
      "pros": [
        "Highly extensible without modifying core code",
        "Scales well to many forge implementations",
        "Macro-driven registration reduces boilerplate",
        "Enables optional compilation of forge modules",
        "Future-proof for community-contributed forges"
      ],
      "cons": [
        "Procedural macro complexity and compilation overhead",
        "Steeper learning curve for contributors",
        "Requires careful macro design to avoid errors",
        "May be over-engineered if only 4 forges needed"
      ],
      "complexity": "complex",
      "files_touched": 18
    }
  ],
  "recommendation": "Approach 1 (Trait-based Forge Abstraction Layer) is recommended. It balances elegance, maintainability, and Rust idioms. The ForgeProvider trait cleanly abstracts common operations while allowing each implementation to handle forge-specific details. This approach is idiomatic Rust, easy to test with mocks, and scales to additional forges without core changes. It avoids over-engineering (Approach 3) while being more maintainable than Approach 2's enum dispatch. Start with a minimal trait covering essential operations (list_repos, get_repo, create_webhook, fetch_pull_requests) and extend based on feature requirements. Use feature flags to make each forge optional, reducing binary size for users who only need specific providers."
}
